agent_raw: The hospital quality team's mortality comparison cannot establish that the sepsis risk model improved care. The analysis compares November-December (model active) against a July-September historical cohort — two structurally different seasons in emergency medicine. Winter emergency presentations differ from summer in illness type, acuity, and mortality risk. This seasonal case-mix difference alone could explain the observed mortality improvement regardless of model deployment. The problem is compounded by two concurrent care changes: a winter surge unit opened and empiric antibiotic guidance was updated at the same time the model went live. Antibiotic timing and selection are among the strongest determinants of sepsis mortality — a protocol update is a plausible primary cause of mortality improvement, not a background variable. Finally, the analysis cohort is defined by early lactate ordering, which is a clinician-initiated behavior that may have expanded after the model or the protocol update changed clinical workflows. If more or different patients received lactate tests during the model-active period, the comparison cohorts are not equivalent. The combination of a before-after cross-seasonal design, three concurrent interventions, and cohort selection sensitivity makes the current analysis insufficient to support hospital-wide extension. At minimum, a prior-year November-December comparison should be conducted; a concurrent control unit that received the antibiotic protocol update without the model alert would further strengthen the evaluation.
all_issues_raised: Seasonal case-mix confound between November-December and July-September comparison periods
Concurrent antibiotic protocol update and winter surge unit as competing mortality interventions
Lactate ordering criterion selection sensitive to clinician behavior changes after model deployment
